learning_outcomes.description: Identify and describe the major forms of corrosion, their underlying causes and mechanisms.
Apply the key thermodynamic principles to predict conditions under which metals corrode, using the Nernst equation, Electromotive Force (EMF) and Galvanic Series, and Pourbaix diagrams.
Analyse and apply kinetic concepts by using mixed potential theory and polarisation curves to predict corrosion current and corrosion potential.
Demonstrate a familiarity with the key testing methods for measuring corrosion and the proficiency to estimate corrosion rates from relevant test data.
Evaluate the nature and limitations of passivity in metals and its influence on corrosion resistance.
Explain the interaction between corrosion and stress in causing failure of materials.
Demonstrate familiarity with industry-relevant corrosion mitigation principles and techniques for practical engineering applications.
Design corrosion protection systems for assets exposed to corrosive environments by applying key concepts of corrosion science.
Research and analyse a corrosion‑related topic and deliver an oral presentation that effectively communicates your findings, individually or in a group.
Identify and assess real-world corrosion issues and apply corrosion knowledge to develop a comprehensive written technical report.

assessments.due_date:
Week 2 Wed - Week 12 Fri

The online quiz begins on Wednesday at 12 pm and should be completed by Friday at 4 pm of the relevant week.
assessments.task_description:
There will be weekly online quizzes (a minimum of 3 questions) from week 2 to week 12. Each quiz will assess the material covered in that specific week.

The quiz must be completed in a single sitting within 25 minutes and submitted before 4:00 pm on the Friday of the corresponding week.

Quiz formats may include a mix of multiple-choice, numerical, and short-answer questions. 

There will be 11 quizzes in total. The marks from the 9 best quizzes will be averaged to determine the overall quiz mark.

This assessment task is non-invigilated. Marking criteria will be available on the Blackboard Ultra site.

Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance.

Failure to reference the use of generative AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
assessments.submission_guidelines: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.late_submission: You will receive a mark of 0 if this assessment is submitted late.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.due_date:
Week 5 Mon - Week 12 Fri

Students are required to submit a copy of their presentation by 5 pm on Friday, the week before they are scheduled to present.
assessments.task_description:
Students are required to deliver a presentation on key aspects of corrosion. These presentations are an integral component of the course and will take place during weeks 5 to 12. 

The aim of this assessment is for students to critically evaluate and present a designated corrosion topic, as well as evaluate presentations delivered by their peers. This task is also designed to enhance students' communication and presentation skills. Presentations may be delivered individually or as part of a group.

Each presentation should be 7-10 min long, followed by 5 min for questions. The text (Jones) should be considered only as a starting point, and students are expected to consult additional resources. 

Students are required to upload their PowerPoint presentation to Blackboard Ultra one week prior to their scheduled presentation (i.e., by 5 pm on the Friday of the preceding week of their presentation). Uploaded presentations will be made available to the rest of the class. All students are expected to have read the relevant text and reviewed the uploaded presentations before class. Each class member is also required to actively participate by asking at least one relevant question during the session.

The course coordinator or a designated panel will award marks following a standard rubric. Assessment is based on Technical content (30%) and Presentation (70%).

Please refer to Blackboard Ultra for the detailed marking criteria.

This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of the assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance.

A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.

To pass this assessment, students must demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission (slides or posters), independent of AI and MT tools.

assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
Students are required to submit a technical report that explains a range of corrosion cases. The purpose of this assessment is for students to apply the fundamental corrosion concepts taught in the course and to communicate their evaluations in a clear and structured written format. 

The report may be submitted either individually or in pairs. If the report is submitted as a group, the title page must include the names and student numbers of both group members. Both students must also confirm that the report was prepared collaboratively and that each member contributed equally to the work. If, for any reason, you find that your group is not functioning effectively, you are encouraged to contact the Course Coordinator for assistance and support.

Specific instructions for the report are as follows:

  • Students will be provided with a set of scenarios or tasks related to specific corrosion cases.
  • The report should be a concise document of about 6-12 pages (12pt font, 1.5 line spacing, maximum 6000 words), including 3-6 relevant diagrams for each case.
  • Explanations should be clear and easy to understand within the given context.
  • Technical details must be presented accurately but in a manner understandable to a non-technical person.  

Full assignment details will be released on Blackboard Ultra in Week 4. Submitting a report with a low similarity score is essential.

Marks are earned for Technical content (70%) and Presentation (30%).

Please refer to Blackboard for detailed marking criteria.

Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance.

A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Assignment is to be in e-format via Turnitin on Blackboard Ultra.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
Format: Short answer, Short essay, Problem solving

The final examination provides an opportunity for students to demonstrate their mastery of the course learning objectives, including:

  • An in-depth understanding of corrosion and its mitigation principles
  • The ability to apply knowledge, including the use of critical judgment
  • The ability to communicate technical information clearly in written form

The examination will assess material covered throughout the entire course.

Questions may touch on the following concepts:

  • Design and application of corrosion knowledge in engineering contexts
  • Explanation of key learning objectives (KLO) related to corrosion
  • Terminology: describing, discussing, or defining corrosion‑related terms

For essay-type questions, students are expected to provide clear, logically-structured, and well-reasoned explanations.

This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempt to use AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.

learning_activities.topic:
Guided Learning

Students work in their own time to go through the textbook. The respective chapters for reading each week will be found in Blackboard. To supplement learning, students will have the opportunity to ask questions regarding content on the discussion board on Blackboard, during lectures and tutorials, or by other means of communication with course staff if necessary (e.g. email).

Student Presentations

Students will give short presentations (7-10 min) on an assigned corrosion topic from Week 5-12. A 5-min Q&A session is reserved at the end of each presentation to give opportunities for students to ask questions. Recordings of the presentation will be made available to students.
